title: Portability export integrity — signed manifest verification
exclusions: Does NOT verify whether the export format is machine-readable (Art.20 — format scoring is a content review handled outside Splunk).
evidence: Hourly search 'portability_manifest_verify' hashes each portability export bundle and cross-checks against the signed manifest stored in compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.20 grants the right to receive 'in a structured, commonly used and machine-readable format'; the UC verifies export integrity so the bundle the subject receives matches what was generated.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.20 (Right to data portability) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.36.3: Portability export integrity — signed manifest verification.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.36.3' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Derived from GDPR@2016/679 Art.20 (parent assurance='full'). UK GDPR preserves parent clause numbering; inherited assurance degraded one step.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that UK GDPR Art.20 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.36.3: Portability export integrity — signed manifest verification.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.36.3' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a portability export bundle's SHA-256 does not match its signed manifest at delivery time, the UC fires naming the export_id, the subject_token, and the divergent hash.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Normal re-hashing during CDN-assisted delivery (content-encoding=gzip) where the manifest carries the pre-compression hash does NOT fire.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=dsar sourcetype=portability:export action=delivered earliest=-30d
| join export_id [search index=dsar sourcetype=portability:manifest | fields export_id, manifest_hash]
| where delivered_hash!=manifest_hash
| table _time export_id subject_token delivered_hash manifest_hash
description: Validates that the bytes delivered to a data subject match the signed manifest produced when the export was generated. Any divergence is evidence of in-transit tampering or a broken CDN path; either way the export cannot be trusted as a lawful Art.20 response.
value: Adds cryptographic evidence to what is otherwise a 'we generated the file' audit trail.
implementation: (1) Sign manifests at export time with an HSM key scoped to the privacy program; (2) log manifest_hash and delivered_hash at each delivery; (3) run hourly; (4) on hit, auto-open an Art.12 remediation ticket. Indexes required: `index=dsar`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=portability:export`, `sourcetype=portability:manifest`.
visualization: Table of failures, single-value for '% verified (30d)', time chart of failures. Key fields: `delivered_hash`, `export_id`, `manifest_hash`, `subject_token`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `dsar` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.20`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.20 (Right to data portability) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.36.3: Portability export integrity — signed manifest verification." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `dsar` under `portability:export`, `portability:manifest`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Sign manifests at export time with an HSM key scoped to the privacy program. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 2:** log manifest_hash and delivered_hash at each delivery. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** run hourly. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** on hit, auto-open an Art.12 remediation ticket. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Portability export integrity — signed manifest verification," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the GDPR control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`dsar`, sourcetype=`portability:export`) with a subsearch on `export_id` across the last 30 days. `type=inner` means the search only returns rows where `export_id` exists in both datasets. This is the correct choice when the detection requires presence in both sources — a left-join would include orphaned rows that cannot be evaluated against the compliance rule.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.36.3: Portability export integrity — signed manifest verification]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.36.3,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=dsar sourcetype=portability:export earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `delivered_hash`, `export_id`, `manifest_hash`, `subject_token`. Run:

```spl
index=dsar sourcetype=portability:export earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, delivered_hash, export_id, manifest_hash, subject_token
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.36.3: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Failures (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Single-Value For '% Verified (30D)' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Time Chart Of Failures (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see when data-subject requests are running late so privacy duties stay on track across systems.
